---
title: "Glycerol (INS 422)"
slug: glycerol-ins-422
category: "Additives & Functional"
category-slug: additives-functional
keywords: ["Glycerol", "Glycerin", "Humectant", "Polyol", "INS 422", "Food Additive", "Moisture Retention"]
description: "Glycerol (INS 422), also commonly known as glycerin or glycerine, is a simple polyol compound discovered in 1779 by Carl Wilhelm Scheele"
---

## History and Sourcing

[Glycerol (INS 422)](glycerol-ins-422.qmd), also commonly known as glycerin or glycerine, is a simple polyol compound discovered in 1779 by Carl Wilhelm Scheele. It is naturally present in all triglycerides, the primary components of animal fats and vegetable oils. In India, its sourcing is linked to the edible oil and oleochemical industries, where it is obtained as a by-product of soap manufacturing and biodiesel production. Its name derives from the Greek word 'glykys,' meaning sweet, reflecting its mildly sweet taste.

## Home Kitchen Use

In the Indian home kitchen, pure glycerol is not a staple ingredient for direct culinary application, though its presence is inherent in many traditional foods rich in fats and oils. Modern home bakers and confectioners might use food-grade glycerol to enhance moisture retention in cakes, fondants, or icings, preventing them from drying out and improving texture. Its subtle sweetness can also balance flavours in certain sweet preparations.

## Industrial Applications

Industrially, glycerol (INS 422) is a highly versatile food additive in the FMCG sector. Its primary function is as a humectant, retaining moisture in products like baked goods, confectionery, dried fruits, and processed meats, extending shelf life and maintaining texture. It serves as a solvent for flavourings and food colours, ensuring even dispersion. Glycerol also acts as a plasticizer, thickener, and stabilizer, contributing to product consistency and mouthfeel. Its mild sweetness and low caloric value make it suitable for dietetic and low-sugar products.

## Distinctions and Common Confusion

Consumers often encounter glycerol without fully understanding its nature, sometimes conflating it with fats due to its origin. It is crucial to distinguish glycerol as a sugar alcohol or polyol, not a fat, despite being derived from triglycerides. While it contributes to texture and moisture, it functions differently from fats. It is also distinct from other humectants or sugar substitutes. The terms 'glycerin' and 'glycerine' are common synonyms for glycerol, referring to the same compound.
